name: BiMetal Gold Mine
short_description: Historic gold mining area on BLM public land near Kingman. Pan for gold and collect quartz specimens in a classic Arizona desert rockhounding site with easy access.
description:
The BiMetal Gold Mine area sits on Arizona Bureau of Land Management property near Kingman, offering rockhounders a chance to explore historic mining terrain and search for gold in the surrounding washes and outcrops. The site sits at 3030 feet elevation in the Mojave Desert, where weathered granite and quartz veins weather out along ridgelines and in dry washes. This area was part of the regional gold mining boom of the late 1800s and early 1900s, and evidence of old mining activity remains visible in the landscape.

Gold specimens and small flakes can be found by panning in the washes during and after seasonal rains when water flow exposes fresh material. Quartz is abundant throughout the area, often in white or milky varieties that fluoresce under UV light. The terrain is relatively open desert with scattered creosote bush and small rocks, making it easy to cover ground and search multiple areas.

This BLM site is beginner-friendly and requires no permit for personal use collection. The proximity to Kingman (just 3 miles away) makes it accessible for day trips, and the moderate elevation means it is comfortable to visit during most of the year. Weather can be extreme in summer, but late fall through spring offers ideal rockhounding conditions with moderate temperatures and occasional desert rains that improve collecting conditions.
history: The BiMetal Gold Mine area lies within the Mojave Desert region where Precambrian granitic bedrock has been mineralized by hydrothermal activity associated with ancient fault zones. Quartz veins formed as silica-rich fluids cooled along fractures, while gold deposits accumulated as placer material in washes downstream from eroded veins. Historic mining activity in the late 1800s and early 1900s focused on small-scale gold extraction from these accessible deposits.
tips: Focus your search in dry washes where water flow has concentrated heavy minerals including gold and hematite - the inside bends of washes are prime spots. Bring a small gold pan and test material from gravel bars; even small amounts of flour gold and fine flakes are common here. Look for white or translucent quartz outcrops on ridgelines and collect samples where they weather free from the host rock. Early morning is best to avoid afternoon heat and to search while temperatures are manageable for desert fieldwork.
terrain_notes: Elevation 3030 feet with moderate slopes and open desert terrain dominated by creosote bush and scattered rocks. Walking is straightforward on relatively flat areas near washes, though footing becomes uneven on rocky hillsides. Parking is available at pullouts near Shinarump Road, and most productive areas are within a short walk (under 1 mile) of parking. No shade is available, so sun exposure is significant.
best_season: October - April. Desert heat peaks June through August, making midday rockhounding dangerous. Winter months offer comfortable temperatures, and occasional winter rains improve collecting conditions in washes. Late fall and early spring (October-November and March-April) combine ideal temperatures with dry conditions suitable for exploring rocky terrain.
written_directions: From Kingman, take US 66 Historic (Shinarump Road) west toward the BiMetal Gold Mine area for approximately 2 miles. Look for BLM access points and pullouts on the north or south side of the road near mile marker references. GPS coordinates 35.155909, -114.081909 will lead directly to the site. High-clearance vehicles are not required for main access roads, but some rough dirt spurs may benefit from AWD.
road_conditions: Shinarump Road is paved; BLM access roads are dirt or gravel with minimal washboard in most seasons. Suitable for passenger vehicles with standard clearance.
parking_notes: Dirt pullouts available near Shinarump Road. Parking is informal on BLM land; expect limited improvements. Capacity for several vehicles at each pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No vehicles off established roads. Pack out all trash. Respect any posted closures or seasonal restrictions.

nearest_services: Kingman, Arizona, 3 miles away. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging. Hospital and emergency services available in Kingman.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain and easy walking near parking areas and washes. Most productive collecting is within 1 mile of parking pullouts on level or gently sloping ground. Uneven rocky terrain on hillsides. Sturdy footwear recommended. No wheelchair accessibility on BLM trails, but road-side collecting is possible for limited mobility visitors.

faq.question: Can you find gold at BiMetal Gold Mine near Kingman?
faq.answer: Yes, small amounts of placer gold and flour gold are consistently found in washes at BiMetal Gold Mine. Panning gravel from wash bottoms and inside bends often yields fine gold flakes. The site is a historic mining area, so gold is present in accessible quantities for personal collection.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at BiMetal Gold Mine in Arizona?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at BiMetal Gold Mine. It is located on BLM public land where recreational collecting is allowed without permits under FLPMA guidelines. You must follow the 25 lb per day limit and pack out all trash.

faq.question: What is the best season to find gold near Kingman, Arizona?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for rockhounding at BiMetal Gold Mine. Desert heat makes summer unsafe for fieldwork. Winter rains can improve collecting by moving material in washes, and cool temperatures make extended searching comfortable. Late fall and early spring offer the best combination of good weather and water flow.

faq.question: What tools do you need to pan for gold at BiMetal Gold Mine?
faq.answer: A small gold pan (10-14 inches) is essential for testing material and concentrating gold flakes. Bring a small shovel or trowel to sample wash gravel, a 5-gallon bucket, and a snuffer bottle to recover fine gold. A UV light can help spot some quartz specimens after dark.

faq.question: Is BiMetal Gold Mine rockhounding site beginner friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, BiMetal Gold Mine is ideal for beginners. The terrain is open and easy to walk, no permit is required, and gold is present in recoverable amounts. Panning is simple to learn, and the site is only 3 miles from Kingman with full services available. No special experience is needed to enjoy collecting here.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can you collect at BiMetal Gold Mine?
faq.answer: Gold and quartz are the primary collectibles at BiMetal Gold Mine. White and milky quartz specimens are abundant on hillsides and in washes. Some specimens fluoresce under UV light. Small amounts of hematite and other iron minerals also occur as byproducts of panning. The site focuses on gold and quartz rather than gemstones, making it a good educational location for learning about ore minerals.

meta_description: Find gold and quartz at BiMetal Gold Mine near Kingman, Arizona. BLM public land, no permit required, beginner friendly. Pan for placer gold and collect quartz specimens.
